critic_raw:
The projection-based truncated SVD update algorithm claims reduced computational overhead by avoiding full matrix decompositions. The primary claimed advantage is computational efficiency — but the computational savings of the projection approach depend on the fraction of singular vectors that need updating relative to the full decomposition cost. No analysis bounds the computational advantage as a function of the update size or the ratio of changed entries to total matrix size. The efficiency claim requires this characterization to be quantitative.

The algorithm is described as maintaining the accuracy of the truncated SVD under matrix evolution. Truncated SVD is an approximation of the full SVD, and the approximation error introduced by the projection-based update accumulates over successive updates. No analysis of how approximation error accumulates over multiple evolution steps is provided; the algorithm may be accurate for a single update but drift substantially from the true truncated SVD after many updates.

The evaluation compares the proposed algorithm against baseline methods on a set of test cases. The selection of baselines and test cases determines whether the comparison is fair. If baselines are weakly implemented or test cases are selected from scenarios where the projection approach has a structural advantage, the reported improvements may not be representative. The paper's claims require independent reproducibility against well-implemented baselines across diverse matrix types.
all_issues_raised: Computational savings are not analytically bounded as a function of update size or matrix sparsity; the efficiency claim is not quantitative.
Approximation error accumulation over multiple successive updates is not analyzed; accuracy guarantees after many updates are uncharacterized.
Baseline selection and test case diversity are not characterized; reported improvements may reflect favorable experimental design.

round_transcripts.critic:
The projection-based truncated SVD update algorithm claims reduced computational overhead by avoiding full matrix decompositions. The primary claimed advantage is computational efficiency — but the computational savings of the projection approach depend on the fraction of singular vectors that need updating relative to the full decomposition cost. No analysis bounds the computational advantage as a function of the update size or the ratio of changed entries to total matrix size. The efficiency claim requires this characterization to be quantitative.

The algorithm is described as maintaining the accuracy of the truncated SVD under matrix evolution. Truncated SVD is an approximation of the full SVD, and the approximation error introduced by the projection-based update accumulates over successive updates. No analysis of how approximation error accumulates over multiple evolution steps is provided; the algorithm may be accurate for a single update but drift substantially from the true truncated SVD after many updates.

The evaluation compares the proposed algorithm against baseline methods on a set of test cases. The selection of baselines and test cases determines whether the comparison is fair. If baselines are weakly implemented or test cases are selected from scenarios where the projection approach has a structural advantage, the reported improvements may not be representative. The paper's claims require independent reproducibility against well-implemented baselines across diverse matrix types.
round_transcripts.defender:
The efficiency characterization concern is fair, but empirical timing and complexity comparisons across different matrix sizes and update magnitudes are the standard way to characterize computational advantages in the algorithms literature. If the paper provides empirical runtime comparisons across a range of problem scales, the lack of a closed-form bound is a theoretical gap but not an empirical one.

On error accumulation: the algorithm's design incorporates re-orthogonalization steps precisely to manage the drift that would otherwise accumulate. Whether these steps are sufficient to bound error over many updates is an important question, but the methodology is not oblivious to the problem. The question is whether the paper includes multi-step experiments or theoretical error bounds.

The baseline concern is generic to all empirical comparisons. If the paper uses standard algorithmic baselines (e.g., recomputing SVD from scratch, incremental SVD) and tests on matrices with diverse structural properties, the comparison is fair by community standards.

round_transcripts.critic:
The efficiency defense accepts the gap between theoretical bounds and empirical characterization. The residual is whether the empirical timing results span sufficient problem scales to characterize the regime where the projection approach outperforms recomputation — if all experiments are at a scale where full SVD is expensive, the advantage may disappear at smaller scales. The range of experimental scales determines the quantitative scope of the efficiency claim.

The error accumulation defense — that re-orthogonalization steps manage drift — is reasonable but requires verification. The critic does not dispute that re-orthogonalization is included; the question is whether it is sufficient and how often it needs to be applied. If the paper specifies the re-orthogonalization frequency and characterizes error under different frequencies, the concern is addressed. Without this characterization, the multi-update accuracy claim is unquantified.

The baseline concern is accepted as genre-standard if the paper uses established baselines with appropriate implementations.
round_transcripts.defender: The experimental scale range is a real gap in characterizing when the efficiency claim holds. If all experiments are at large scales, the break-even point with recomputation is unknown. The re-orthogonalization frequency and its effect on error accumulation is accepted as a needed characterization. The baseline point is accepted as adequately addressed if established methods are used.

all_issues_adjudicated: Efficiency break-even scale is not characterized; the regime where projection update outperforms recomputation is unquantified.
Re-orthogonalization frequency and its effect on multi-step approximation error are not characterized.
